does: This paper investigates whether sequential post-training of large language models causes representation collapse—a phenomenon where internal representations become increasingly low-rank, anisotropic, and homogeneous across training stages. The authors develop a comprehensive measurement suite to quantify these geometric properties across hidden states, logits, token trajectories, and LoRA updates, then systematically analyze collapse across multiple post-training scenarios including supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, safety tuning, and task specialization. They establish that representation collapse correlates with reduced plasticity for subsequent fine-tuning, degraded out-of-domain generalization, and worse calibration. The paper additionally proposes and evaluates lightweight interventions—mixed-domain replay, feature refresh, representation diversity regularization, and LoRA decorrelation—that mitigate collapse while preserving the behavioral improvements gained through post-training stages.
connects: This work builds on foundational research into the geometry and structure of neural network representations, extending prior work on representation learning, feature collapse, and redundancy in deep learning. It responds to growing empirical observations that multi-stage post-training pipelines sometimes yield unexpected degradations in model capabilities, connecting to literature on catastrophic forgetting, task interference, and the plasticity-stability tradeoff in continual learning. The paper leverages tools from dimensionality analysis and representation quality metrics developed in earlier work on neural network interpretability and feature spaces. It also engages with recent research on parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and their interaction with sequential adaptation, as well as studies examining how preference optimization and alignment procedures reshape model internals. The measurement framework builds on prior work quantifying anisotropy and isotropy in learned representations.
enables: This research enables more robust design of multi-stage training pipelines by providing diagnostic tools to identify when and how representation collapse occurs. The interventions proposed—particularly feature refresh and diversity regularization—offer practical techniques for practitioners to maintain model plasticity across sequential post-training stages, potentially allowing longer adaptation chains without performance degradation. The work opens directions for developing post-training schedules that balance behavioral alignment goals with representation health. It also motivates future investigation into the fundamental tradeoffs between task-specific adaptation and generality, and suggests that geometric properties of representations may be important optimization constraints for training procedures. The paper points toward potential benefits of diversity-aware training objectives and mixed-replay strategies in continual learning settings more broadly, relevant to both language models and other domains requiring sequential adaptation.
